content: How does Psalm 23 begin?
explanation: Scripture says: 'The LORD is my shepherd' (Psalm 23:1).

content: In Psalm 23, when walking through the valley of the shadow of death, why does the author not fear?
explanation: Scripture says: 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me' (Psalm 23:4).
options: Because His rod and staff comfort me

content: In the first Psalm, who is described as blessed?
explanation: Scripture says: 'Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the wicked, nor stands in the way of sinners, nor sits in the seat of scoffers' (Psalm 1:1).
options: The one who does not walk in the counsel of the wicked
The one who does not stand in the way of sinners
The one who does not sit in the seat of scoffers

content: What does Psalm 100 call us to do?
explanation: Scripture says: 'Make a joyful noise to the LORD, all the earth!' (Psalm 100:1).
options: Make a joyful noise to the LORD

content: Whom does Psalm 91 say is protected?
explanation: Scripture says: 'He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty' (Psalm 91:1).
options: He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High

content: What does Psalm 46 say God is?
explanation: Scripture says: 'God is our refuge and strength' (Psalm 46:1).

content: Psalm 23 begins 'The LORD is my shepherd.' What does the Hebrew word for 'shepherd' mean?
explanation: Raah (רעה) means 'to shepherd, to lead' — not just to protect but to guide and care.

content: In Psalm 46:10, 'Be still and know that I am God.' What does the Hebrew word for 'be still' mean?
explanation: Rapha (רפה) means 'to let go, to release' — not just be silent but release anxiety.
options: Rapha — to let go, to release

content: How does Psalm 23 begin?
explanation: Psalm 23:1 — 'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.' One of the most beloved psalms.
options: The heavens declare the glory of God

content: To what does Psalm 1 compare the righteous?
explanation: Psalm 1:3 — 'He is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season, and its leaf does not wither.' All he does prospers.
options: A tree planted by streams of water yielding fruit in its season

content: Psalm 46:10 says: 'Be still and know that I am ___'
explanation: Psalm 46:10 — A call to be still in God's presence, trusting that He is in control.

content: To what does Psalm 119:105 compare God's Word?
explanation: Psalm 119:105 — 'Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.' Illuminating direction for life.
options: A lamp to the feet and a light to the path

content: How does Psalm 139:14 say we are made?
explanation: Psalm 139:14 — 'I praise You, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made.' God's work is marvelous.

content: Psalm 22 begins: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' Who quoted this verse from the cross?
explanation: Matthew 27:46 — Jesus quoted Psalm 22:1 while crucified. Psalm 22 is a prophetic psalm with detailed depictions of the crucifixion.

content: How far does Psalm 103:12 say God removes our sins?
explanation: East and west never meet — sin is utterly removed.
options: As far as the east is from the west
To the ends of the earth

content: What do the heavens declare in Psalm 19:1?
explanation: Psalm 19:1 — 'The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.' Nature is evidence of the Creator.

content: Psalm 34:8: '___ and see that the LORD is good!'
explanation: Psalm 34:8 — Taste (experience) the goodness of the Lord. Faith requires personal experience.

content: Psalm 37:4: '___ yourself in the LORD,' and He will give you the desires of your heart
explanation: Psalm 37:4 — When we delight in the Lord, He shapes our desires to align with His will.

content: Psalm 91 describes the one who shelters in the LORD as protected from what?
explanation: Psalm 91:3-10 — A psalm about God's all-encompassing protection for those who trust Him.
options: All: pestilence, the arrow, terror, plague

content: From where does the psalmist's help come, according to Psalm 121:1-2?
explanation: Psalm 121:1-2 — 'I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come? My help comes from the LORD, who made heaven and earth.'
options: From the LORD, who made heaven and earth

content: Why do nations rage in Psalm 2:7 — God says to His Anointed: 'You are my ___; today I have begotten you.'
explanation: Psalm 2:7 — 'I will tell of the decree: The LORD said to me, You are my Son; today I have begotten you.'

content: Psalm 8:4 asks: 'What is man that you are mindful of him?'
explanation: Psalm 8:4 — 'What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?'
options: What is man that you are mindful of him

content: Psalm 14:1 — What does the fool say in his heart?
explanation: Psalm 14:1 — 'The fool says in his heart, There is no God.'

content: Psalm 16:11 — 'You make known to me ___; in your presence there is fullness of joy.'
explanation: Psalm 16:11 — 'You make known to me the path of life; in your presence there is fullness of joy.'

content: Psalm 18:2 describes the LORD as what?
explanation: Psalm 18:2 — 'The LORD is my rock and my fortress and my deliverer, my God, my rock, in whom I take refuge.'

content: Psalm 19:1 — 'The heavens declare ___ of God, and the sky above proclaims ___.'
explanation: Psalm 19:1 — 'The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.'

content: Psalm 23:1 opens with which famous declaration?
explanation: Psalm 23:1 — 'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.'
options: The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want

content: Psalm 24:7 calls for what to be lifted up so the King of glory may come in?
explanation: Psalm 24:7 — 'Lift up your heads, O gates! And be lifted up, O ancient doors, that the King of glory may come in.'

content: Psalm 27:1 — 'The LORD is ___ and my salvation; whom shall I fear?'
explanation: Psalm 27:1 — 'The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear?'

content: Psalm 30:5 — 'Weeping may tarry for the night, but ___ comes with the morning.'
explanation: Psalm 30:5 — 'Weeping may tarry for the night, but joy comes with the morning.'

content: Psalm 32:1 says blessed is whom?
explanation: Psalm 32:1 — 'Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered.'
options: The one whose transgression is forgiven and sin covered
The one who offers many sacrifices

content: Psalm 34:8 invites us to do what concerning the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 34:8 — 'Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good! Blessed is the man who takes refuge in him!'
options: Taste and see that the LORD is good

content: Psalm 37:4 — 'Delight yourself in the LORD, and he will give you ___'
explanation: Psalm 37:4 — 'Delight yourself in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart.'

content: Psalm 40:1 — What did the writer do before being heard?
explanation: Psalm 40:1 — 'I waited patiently for the LORD; he inclined to me and heard my cry.'

content: Psalm 42:1 — 'As a deer pants for ___, so my soul pants for you, O God.'
explanation: Psalm 42:1 — 'As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God.'

content: Psalm 46:1 — 'God is our ___ and strength.'
explanation: Psalm 46:1 — 'God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble.'

content: Psalm 46:10 — '___ and know that I am God.'
explanation: Psalm 46:10 — 'Be still, and know that I am God. I will be exalted among the nations, I will be exalted in the earth!'

content: Psalm 51:10 — David prayed for God to 'create in me a ___'
explanation: Psalm 51:10 — 'Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me.'

content: Psalm 51:17 — What sacrifices does God accept?
explanation: Psalm 51:17 — 'The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise.'
options: A broken spirit; a contrite and broken heart

content: Psalm 55:22 invites us to do what with our burden?
explanation: Psalm 55:22 — 'Cast your burden on the LORD, and he will sustain you; he will never permit the righteous to be moved.'
options: Cast your burden on the LORD — He will sustain you
Carry it alone to the end

content: Psalm 62:5 — On whom alone does my soul wait?
explanation: Psalm 62:5 — 'For God alone, O my soul, wait in silence, for my hope is from him.'
options: For God alone (my hope is from Him)

content: Psalm 63:1 — David cries: 'My soul ___ for You, my flesh faints for You, in a dry and weary land.'
explanation: Psalm 63:1 — 'O God, you are my God; earnestly I seek you; my soul thirsts for you; my flesh faints for you, as in a dry and weary land where there is no water.'

content: Psalm 73:25 — 'Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I ___'
explanation: Psalm 73:25 — 'Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you.'

content: Psalm 84:10 — A day in God's courts is better than how many days elsewhere?
explanation: Psalm 84:10 — 'For a day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere. I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of wickedness.'

content: Psalm 86:5 describes God as what?
explanation: Psalm 86:5 — 'For you, O Lord, are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love to all who call upon you.'
options: Good, forgiving, abounding in steadfast love

content: Psalm 90 (of Moses) prays God teach us to number what?
explanation: Psalm 90:12 — 'So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom.'
options: Our days — that we may get a heart of wisdom

content: Psalm 91:1 — He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will ___?
explanation: Psalm 91:1 — 'He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty.'
options: Abide in the shadow of the Almighty

content: Psalm 95:6 invites us to do what before our Maker?
explanation: Psalm 95:6 — 'Oh come, let us worship and bow down; let us kneel before the LORD, our Maker!'
options: Come, worship, bow down, and kneel

content: Psalm 96:1 — 'Sing ___ to the LORD.'
explanation: Psalm 96:1 — 'Oh sing to the LORD a new song; sing to the LORD, all the earth!'

content: Psalm 100:3 — Know that the LORD is God; it is He who ___
explanation: Psalm 100:3 — 'Know that the LORD, he is God! It is he who made us, and we are his; we are his people, and the sheep of his pasture.'
options: Made us — we are His people, sheep of His pasture

content: Psalm 103:12 — How far has God removed our transgressions from us?
explanation: Psalm 103:12 — 'As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us.'
options: As far as east is from west

content: Psalm 103:13 — The LORD shows compassion to those who fear Him as whom?
explanation: Psalm 103:13 — 'As a father shows compassion to his children, so the LORD shows compassion to those who fear him.'
options: A father shows compassion to his children

content: Psalm 110:1 (Messianic) — 'The LORD says to my Lord: ___ until I make your enemies your footstool.'
explanation: Psalm 110:1 — 'The LORD says to my Lord: Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool.' Frequently quoted in the New Testament about Christ.

content: Psalm 111:10 — 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of ___'
explanation: Psalm 111:10 — 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom; all those who practice it have a good understanding.'

content: Psalm 115:3 — Where is God, and what does He do?
explanation: Psalm 115:3 — 'Our God is in the heavens; he does all that he pleases.'
options: In the heavens, and does all He pleases

content: Psalm 116:15 — 'Precious in the sight of the LORD is ___ of his saints.'
explanation: Psalm 116:15 — 'Precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of his saints.'

content: Which is the shortest psalm?
explanation: Psalm 117 has only two verses — the shortest chapter in the Bible and also the middle chapter.

content: Psalm 118:22 — 'The stone that the builders rejected has become ___'
explanation: Psalm 118:22 — 'The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.' Quoted in NT of Christ.

content: Psalm 118:24 — 'This is the day that the LORD has made; ___'
explanation: Psalm 118:24 — 'This is the day that the LORD has made; let us rejoice and be glad in it.'
options: Let us rejoice and be glad
Let us come to the temple

content: Psalm 119:11 — 'I have stored up your word in my heart, that ___'
explanation: Psalm 119:11 — 'I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you.'
options: I might not sin against you

content: Psalm 119:105 — 'Your word is a ___ to my feet and a light to my path.'
explanation: Psalm 119:105 — 'Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path.'

content: Psalm 121:1-2 — From where does the writer's help come?
explanation: Psalm 121:1-2 — 'I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come? My help comes from the LORD, who made heaven and earth.'
options: From the LORD, who made heaven and earth

content: Psalm 122:1 — The writer rejoices because of what?
explanation: Psalm 122:1 — 'I was glad when they said to me, Let us go to the house of the LORD!'
options: Going to the house of the LORD

content: Psalm 126:5 — 'Those who sow with ___ shall reap with shouts of joy.'
explanation: Psalm 126:5 — 'Those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy.'

content: Psalm 127:1 — Unless the LORD builds the house, ___
explanation: Psalm 127:1 — 'Unless the LORD builds the house, those who build it labor in vain. Unless the LORD watches over the city, the watchman stays awake in vain.'
options: Those who build labor in vain

content: Psalm 130:1 — From where does the writer cry to the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 130:1 — 'Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD!'

content: Psalm 133:1 — 'Behold, how good and pleasant it is when ___'
explanation: Psalm 133:1 — 'Behold, how good and pleasant it is when brothers dwell in unity!'

content: Psalm 136 — What refrain is repeated in every verse?
explanation: Psalm 136:1 — 'Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever.' The phrase 'his steadfast love endures forever' is repeated 26 times.

content: Psalm 137:1 — By which rivers did the Jews sit and weep, remembering Zion?
explanation: Psalm 137:1 — 'By the waters of Babylon, there we sat down and wept, when we remembered Zion.'

content: Psalm 139:13 — When did God knit together the writer's inward parts?
explanation: Psalm 139:13 — 'For you formed my inward parts; you knitted me together in my mother's womb.'
options: In the mother's womb — knitted together

content: Psalm 139:23-24 — What did David ask God to do with his heart?
explanation: Psalm 139:23-24 — 'Search me, O God, and know my heart! Try me and know my thoughts! And see if there be any grievous way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting!'
options: Search and know my heart — see if any wicked way, lead me in the way everlasting

content: Psalm 143:10 — What did David ask God to teach him?
explanation: Psalm 143:10 — 'Teach me to do your will, for you are my God! Let your good Spirit lead me on level ground!'

content: Psalm 145:18 — Whom is the LORD near to?
explanation: Psalm 145:18 — 'The LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth.'
options: All who call on Him in truth

content: Psalm 146:3 — Whom should you not put your trust in?
explanation: Psalm 146:3 — 'Put not your trust in princes, in a son of man, in whom there is no salvation.'
options: In princes and the son of man

content: Psalm 147:3 — Whom does the LORD heal?
explanation: Psalm 147:3 — 'He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds.'

content: Psalm 150:6 — What is the closing line of the entire Psalter?
explanation: Psalm 150:6 — 'Let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Praise the LORD!'
options: Let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Hallelujah!

content: Psalm 4:8 — Why does the writer lie down and sleep in peace?
explanation: Psalm 4:8 — 'In peace I will both lie down and sleep; for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety.'
options: The LORD alone makes me dwell in safety

content: Psalm 13:1 — What does David lament?
explanation: Psalm 13:1 — 'How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever? How long will you hide your face from me?'
options: How long will You forget me, hide Your face

content: Psalm 25:4-5 — What does David ask God for?
explanation: Psalm 25:4-5 — 'Make me to know your ways, O LORD; teach me your paths. Lead me in your truth and teach me, for you are the God of my salvation.'
options: Make me know Your ways, teach Your paths and lead in Your truth

content: Psalm 33:9 — By what did God create the universe?
explanation: Psalm 33:9 — 'For he spoke, and it came to be; he commanded, and it stood firm.'
options: By laboring with His hands many days

content: Psalm 50:15 — What does God invite us to do in the day of trouble?
explanation: Psalm 50:15 — 'Call upon me in the day of trouble; I will deliver you, and you shall glorify me.'
options: Call upon Me, I will deliver

content: Psalm 56:8 — Where does God put the writer's tears?
explanation: Psalm 56:8 — 'You have kept count of my tossings; put my tears in your bottle. Are they not in your book?'

content: Psalm 67:1 — What does the writer pray God do, that His way may be known on earth?
explanation: Psalm 67:1-2 — 'May God be gracious to us and bless us and make his face to shine upon us, that your way may be known on earth.'
options: Be gracious, bless, and make His face shine on us

content: Psalm 68:5 — God is the father of whom and the protector of whom?
explanation: Psalm 68:5 — 'Father of the fatherless and protector of widows is God in his holy habitation.'
options: Father of righteous kings, protector of the wealthy
Father of patriarchs, protector of Israel
Father of priests, protector of Levites
Father of the fatherless, protector of widows

content: Psalm 85:10 — What four attributes meet and kiss each other?
explanation: Psalm 85:10 — 'Steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss each other.'

content: Psalm 89:1 — What does the writer sing of forever?
explanation: Psalm 89:1 — 'I will sing of the steadfast love of the LORD, forever; with my mouth I will make known your faithfulness to all generations.'
options: The steadfast love and faithfulness of the LORD

content: Psalm 92:1 — 'It is good to give ___ to the LORD, to sing praises to your name, O Most High.'
explanation: Psalm 92:1 — 'It is good to give thanks to the LORD, to sing praises to your name, O Most High.'

content: Psalm 93:1 — What does the LORD wear as He reigns?
explanation: Psalm 93:1 — 'The LORD reigns; he is robed in majesty; the LORD is robed; he has put on strength as his belt.'

content: Psalm 98:1 — 'Sing to the LORD ___, for he has done marvelous things!'
explanation: Psalm 98:1 — 'Oh sing to the LORD a new song, for he has done marvelous things! His right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation for him.'

content: Psalm 103:8 — What kind of heart does the LORD have?
explanation: Psalm 103:8 — 'The LORD is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love.'
options: Merciful, gracious, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love

content: Psalm 104:24 — The earth is full of what of the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 104:24 — 'O LORD, how manifold are your works! In wisdom have you made them all; the earth is full of your creatures.'

content: Psalm 107:1 — Why give thanks to the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 107:1 — 'Oh give thanks to the LORD, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever!'
options: He is good; His steadfast love endures forever
Because He helps only a few

content: Psalm 112:1 — Blessed is the one who does what?
explanation: Psalm 112:1 — 'Blessed is the man who fears the LORD, who greatly delights in his commandments!'
options: The man who fears the LORD and delights in His commandments

content: Psalm 113:3 — From where to where is the LORD's name to be praised?
explanation: Psalm 113:3 — 'From the rising of the sun to its setting, the name of the LORD is to be praised!'
options: From the rising of the sun to its setting

content: Psalm 119:97 — What does the writer love so greatly?
explanation: Psalm 119:97 — 'Oh how I love your law! It is my meditation all the day.'

content: Psalm 119:130 — The unfolding of God's word does what for the simple?
explanation: Psalm 119:130 — 'The unfolding of your words gives light; it imparts understanding to the simple.'

content: Psalm 119:165 — What does the one who loves God's law have in great measure?
explanation: Psalm 119:165 — 'Great peace have those who love your law; nothing can make them stumble.'
options: Great peace — nothing can make them stumble

content: Psalm 131:2 — To what does David compare his soul?
explanation: Psalm 131:2 — 'But I have calmed and quieted my soul, like a weaned child with its mother; like a weaned child is my soul within me.'
options: A weaned child with its mother

content: Psalm 138:8 — What does David affirm about the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 138:8 — 'The LORD will fulfill his purpose for me; your steadfast love, O LORD, endures forever. Do not forsake the work of your hands.'
options: He will fulfill His purpose for me — His steadfast love endures forever
He will test me to the end
He will give a firstborn son

content: Psalm 141:3 — What does David ask the LORD to set over his mouth?
explanation: Psalm 141:3 — 'Set a guard, O LORD, over my mouth; keep watch over the door of my lips!'
options: A guard — keep watch over my lips

content: Psalm 145:3 — Great is the LORD and greatly to be praised; His greatness is ___
explanation: Psalm 145:3 — 'Great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised, and his greatness is unsearchable.'

content: Psalm 148:1-3 — Who and what are called to praise the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 148:2-3 — 'Praise him, all his angels; praise him, all his hosts! Praise him, sun and moon, praise him, all you shining stars!'

content: Psalm 149:1 — What should we sing to the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 149:1 — 'Praise the LORD! Sing to the LORD a new song, his praise in the assembly of the godly!'
options: A new song — in the assembly of the godly

content: Psalm 150:3-5 — With what instruments should we praise the LORD?
explanation: Psalm 150:3-5 — 'Praise him with trumpet sound; praise him with lute and harp! Praise him with tambourine and dance; praise him with strings and pipe! Praise him with sounding cymbals.'
options: Trumpet, lute, harp, tambourine, dance, strings, pipe, cymbals

content: Psalm 22:1 — The opening line was spoken by Jesus on the cross — what is it?
explanation: Psalm 22:1 — 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' Jesus quoted this on the cross (Matthew 27:46).
options: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?
Into Your hands I commit my spirit

content: Psalm 22:18 — What was prophesied about Jesus' crucifixion?
explanation: Psalm 22:18 — 'They divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' Fulfilled when soldiers cast lots for Jesus' clothing (John 19:23-24).

content: The New Testament frequently quotes Psalms to confirm Christ's ministry. Which Psalms ARE clearly quoted in the NT? (select all correct)
explanation: Psalm 2 quoted in Heb 1:5; Psalm 22 (especially 22:1, 22:18) quoted in the gospels at the crucifixion; Psalm 110 is the most-quoted in NT (Matt 22:44, Heb 1:13, 5:6, 7:17). Psalm 200 does not exist — Psalter has 150 chapters.
options: Psalm 2 — 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you' (Heb 1:5)
Psalm 22 — Messianic lament (quoted in Matt 27 from the cross)
Psalm 110 — 'Sit at my right hand' (Matt 22, Heb 1)
Psalm 200 — Prophecy of the second coming (Psalms has only 150 chapters)

content: Psalm 1 and Psalm 73 both consider 'the wicked' and 'the righteous'. What is the core difference of Psalm 73 from Psalm 1?
explanation: Ps 73:2-12 — Asaph nearly stumbles seeing the wicked prosper. 73:16-17 — 'But when I thought how to understand this, it seemed to me a wearisome task, until I went into THE SANCTUARY of God; then I discerned their end'. The faith crisis is resolved through worship.
options: Psalm 73 denies Psalm 1's view and says the wicked will never be judged at all
Psalm 73 describes a CRISIS of faith over the wicked's prosperity, resolved when entering THE SANCTUARY
Psalm 73 is only about David personally, with no application to Israel as a people
Psalm 73 is identical to Psalm 1, just written in Hebrew instead of Aramaic

content: Psalm 2:7 — 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you'. In the NT, this verse is quoted to demonstrate what about Christ?
explanation: Heb 1:5 — 'For to which of the angels did God ever say, You are my Son, today I have begotten you?' (quoting Ps 2:7). Heb 1:1-14 uses Ps 2:7 to prove the Son's superiority over the angels.
options: Christ is merely the most exalted angel created by the Father
Christ came as a prophet in the line of the OT prophets
Christ is the UNIQUE SON of God, far superior to the angels (Heb 1:5)
Christ is just an ordinary king like David on the earthly throne

content: Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:25-31) quotes Psalm 16:10 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption'. To whom does Peter apply this verse?
explanation: Acts 2:29-31 — 'David... both died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day. Being therefore a prophet... he foresaw and spoke of the resurrection of the Christ, that he was not abandoned to Hades, nor did his flesh see corruption'.
options: To David, because David lives forever and never dies bodily
To every believer, because they will never die physically in this life
To the angels, because they are beings that cannot be corrupted at all
To Christ, because David died and his body decayed, but Christ rose again

content: Psalm 19 has a TWO-PART structure on God's revelation. What is that structure?
explanation: Ps 19:1-6 — 'The heavens declare the glory of God'. Ps 19:7-14 — 'The law of the LORD is perfect'. C.S. Lewis called Psalm 19 'the greatest poem in the Psalter' for its balanced structure: general revelation (creation) and special (Word).
options: Part 1 (19:1-6): Revelation through creation (heavens, sun) — Part 2 (19:7-14): Revelation through the Word (law of the LORD)
Part 1 (19:1-6): The history of Israel — Part 2 (19:7-14): The future Messiah's coming
Part 1 (19:1-6): David's sin — Part 2 (19:7-14): His repentance and restoration
Part 1 (19:1-6): The Zion temple — Part 2 (19:7-14): The sacrifices in the temple

content: Psalm 24:1 — 'The earth is the LORD's and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell therein'. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians to address what practical issue?
explanation: 1 Cor 10:25-26 — 'Eat whatever is sold in the meat market without raising any question on the ground of conscience. For the earth is the Lord's, and the fullness thereof' (citing Ps 24:1). Since all is the Lord's, no food is intrinsically unclean.
options: Divisions in the church between Paul's followers and Apollos's followers
Eating meat sacrificed to idols (1 Cor 10:25-26) — 'everything on earth belongs to the Lord'
Believers marrying unbelievers within the Corinthian church
Believers taking each other to court before pagan Roman judges

content: Psalm 95:7-11 warns: 'Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts as at Meribah'. The author of Hebrews applies this multiple times (Heb 3:7-11; 4:1-11) to teach what?
explanation: Heb 3:7-15 and 4:1-11 expand Psalm 95's 'today': the moment to obey is NOW, not future. The true rest is the spiritual rest in Christ, not merely Joshua's promised land.
options: Keep the Sabbath day with all the OT rituals each week strictly
Fast at least one day per week to enter into rest
Obey TODAY — because a Sabbath rest still remains for the people of God
Travel to the Jerusalem temple at least three times a year by the Law

content: Christian tradition identifies SEVEN specific psalms as the 'Penitential Psalms'. Which psalms ARE in this set of seven? (select all correct)
explanation: The seven traditional Penitential Psalms: 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143. Marked by confession + plea for mercy. Psalm 100 is a joyful praise psalm, not penitential.
options: Psalm 32 — 'Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven' (David after repentance)
Psalm 51 — 'Have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love' (after Bathsheba)
Psalm 130 — 'Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD' (De Profundis)
Psalm 100 — 'Make a joyful noise to the LORD' (a praise psalm, not penitential)

content: Psalms has multiple authors, identified in their titles. Which author-psalm pairs ARE in the book? (select all correct)
explanation: Psalm authors: David (~73 psalms, incl. Ps 51), sons of Korah (Pss 42-49, 84-85, 87-88), Asaph (Pss 50, 73-83), Solomon (Pss 72, 127), Moses (Ps 90), Heman (Ps 88), Ethan (Ps 89). Paul is a NT apostle, not an OT author.
options: David — author of Psalm 51 and many other psalms
Sons of Korah — authors of Psalms 42-49 and others

content: Psalms 32 and 51 are both Davidic penitential psalms following what event in David's life?
explanation: Psalm 51's superscription — 'When Nathan the prophet came to him, after he had gone in to Bathsheba'. 2 Sam 11-12 records adultery with Bathsheba + killing Uriah + Nathan's rebuke. Psalm 32 reflects the corresponding 'confession' experience.
options: After defeating Goliath in the Valley of Elah with just one stone
After the Bathsheba affair and the killing of Uriah (2 Sam 11-12) — Nathan's rebuke
After Absalom's rebellion and David's flight from Jerusalem
After numbering Israel, which brought a plague on the people (2 Sam 24)

content: Two distinct 'shepherd psalms' in the Psalter: Psalm 23 and Psalm 80. What is the core difference?
explanation: Ps 23:1 — 'The LORD is my shepherd' (individual). Ps 80:1 — 'O Shepherd of Israel, you who lead Joseph like a flock' (national/corporate). Ps 80 is a national lament during distress.
options: Psalm 23 — the LORD is shepherd of the INDIVIDUAL ('David as a sheep'); Psalm 80 — the LORD is shepherd of CORPORATE ISRAEL
Psalm 23 is in Hebrew while Psalm 80 is in Aramaic from the exile period
Psalm 23 is by David while Psalm 80 is by a Gentile, added later to the canon
Psalm 23 is a praise psalm while Psalm 80 is a cursing psalm against enemies

content: Among the 'Royal Psalms' (2, 45, 72, 110), which is the MOST quoted in the NT to prove Christ's deity?
explanation: Psalm 110 is the most-quoted OT psalm in the NT: Matt 22:44, Mark 12:36, Luke 20:42, Acts 2:34-35, Heb 1:13, 5:6, 7:17, 7:21. Verse 110:1 proves Christ as David's Lord; verse 110:4 proves his Melchizedekian priesthood.
options: Psalm 2 — about the king set on Zion
Psalm 110 — 'Sit at my right hand' / Melchizedek

content: Among the 'creation psalms' (Pss 8, 19, 104), what makes Psalm 104 DISTINCT from the other two?
explanation: Psalm 104 has 35 verses depicting God as cosmic ruler in detail: he is 'wrapped in light' (104:2), 'rides on the wings of the wind' (104:3), 'makes grass grow' (104:14), feeds 'young lions roaring for prey' (104:21). While Ps 8 highlights humanity's role and Ps 19 highlights revelation, Ps 104 praises God's ongoing providence in detail.
options: Psalm 104 is just three brief verses about cosmic creation in six days only
Psalm 104 denies creation and says the natural world appeared by random chance
Psalm 104 praises DETAILED PROVIDENCE (winds, rain, mountains, sea, beasts, moon)
Psalm 104 focuses only on mankind and ignores all the other creatures of nature

content: The Psalter contains a set of 15 psalms called 'Songs of Ascent' (pilgrimage songs). Which section of Psalms is this?
explanation: Psalms 120-134 (15 psalms) all carry the heading 'A Song of Ascents' (Hebrew: shir hammaaloth). Traditionally, Israelites sang these as they 'went up' to Jerusalem for the three pilgrimage feasts: Passover, Pentecost (Weeks), and Tabernacles.
options: Psalms 120-134 — used by pilgrims going up to Jerusalem for the three annual feasts
Psalms 1-15 — the opening section for new students of the faith
Psalms 90-104 — the section praising God as eternal
Psalms 145-150 — the closing section with five Hallelujah psalms

content: The five closing psalms (146-150) all open and close with 'Hallelujah' (Praise the LORD). Which psalms belong to this set? (select all correct)
explanation: Psalms 146-150 form the 'Final Hallel': each begins and ends with 'Hallelujah'. Psalm 137 is a Babylonian exile psalm (a lament of suffering), not part of the Hallelujah set.
options: Psalm 146 — 'Praise the LORD, O my soul!'
Psalm 148 — All creation, heaven to earth, praises the LORD
Psalm 150 — 'Praise God with every kind of musical instrument'
Psalm 137 — 'By the rivers of Babylon' (an exile lament, no Hallelujah)

content: Lament psalms typically have a characteristic structure. Psalm 13 is a brief example. What is the typical structure of a lament psalm?
explanation: Ps 13 (the shortest lament): vv. 1-2 cry ('How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?'), vv. 3-4 petition ('Consider and answer me, O LORD my God; light up my eyes'), v. 5 trust ('But I have trusted in your steadfast love'), v. 6 praise ('I will sing to the LORD'). Classic structure: turn from lament to trust.
options: Praise → Thanks → Praise continued forever without any changes
Sacrifice → Atonement → Fierce judgment of enemies very severely
Creation → Fall → Redemption → Glory — four clear stages
Cry / complaint → Petition → TURN TO TRUST → Praise / vow of praise

content: 'Imprecatory psalms' call for God's judgment on enemies. Psalm 137 is famous for which imprecation (137:8-9)?
explanation: Ps 137:8-9 — 'O daughter of Babylon, doomed to be destroyed, blessed shall he be who repays you with what you have done to us! Blessed shall he be who takes your little ones and dashes them against the rock!' This is the anguished response of the exiles after Jerusalem's destruction by Babylon (586 BC).
options: Asking God to kill the pagan kings dwelling with Israel in the promised land
Asking God to REPAY Babylon — 'Blessed shall he be who takes your little ones and dashes them against the rock'
Asking God to slay all Assyrian kings for shedding the prophets' blood in the OT
Asking God to destroy Sodom and Gomorrah for their wickedness ages ago

content: Psalm 1:3 describes the righteous as 'a tree planted by streams of water, that yields its fruit in season'. In context (1:1-3), what is the source of this 'vitality'?
explanation: Ps 1:1-3 — 'Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the wicked... but his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night. He is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season'. Meditation on the Word is the 'stream' nourishing the righteous.
options: He is physically strong because he works hard and exercises regularly each day
He happens to be born into a wealthy family within Israel by good fortune
He 'delights in the LAW of the LORD; on his law he meditates day and night'
He has leadership talent so he rises quickly in social rank

content: Psalm 19:7-9 uses SIX titles for God's Word, each with an attribute and result. Which name-attribute pairs ARE in the text? (select all correct)
explanation: Ps 19:7-9 lists 6 name-attribute pairs: (1) law/perfect, (2) testimony/sure, (3) precepts/right, (4) commandment/pure, (5) fear of the LORD/clean, (6) rules/true. Option D ('revelation is hidden, only angels') is not in this text.
options: 'The LAW of the LORD is PERFECT, reviving the soul'
'The TESTIMONY of the LORD is SURE, making wise the simple'
'The PRECEPTS of the LORD are RIGHT, rejoicing the heart'
'The REVELATION of the LORD is HIDDEN, only angels understand'

content: Psalm 23:4 — 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil'. In the structure of Psalm 23, this confidence rests on what basis?
explanation: Ps 23:4 — 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for YOU ARE WITH ME; your rod and your staff, they comfort me'. The basis is not personal strength but God's PRESENCE and his protective shepherd's tools.
options: David has powerful weapons to fight off all enemies around him in every direction
David is promised by God that he will never die a bodily death
The prophets foretold David would be delivered from every danger in his life
'For YOU ARE WITH ME; your rod and your staff, they COMFORT me'

content: Psalm 32:1-2 — 'Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered!' Paul quotes this in Romans 4 to demonstrate what?
explanation: Rom 4:6-8 — 'Just as David also speaks of the blessing of the one to whom God counts righteousness apart from works: Blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven' (citing Ps 32:1-2). Paul uses this to prove justification by faith.
options: Justification by faith (not works) — including forgiveness of sins by grace
The necessity of circumcision for forgiveness under the Abrahamic covenant
The necessity of yearly sin offerings at the Jerusalem temple
The necessity of repentance before God forgives sins permanently

content: Psalm 51:5 — 'Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me'. What does this verse teach about the human condition?
explanation: Ps 51:5 teaches the doctrine of 'original sin': sin is not just isolated acts but a human CONDITION from conception. David is not accusing his mother; he confesses that man is born in sin (cf. Rom 5:12-19).
options: David accuses his mother of sinning while she was conceiving him in the womb
Sin is not merely an action but a CONDITION present from conception (the root of sin)
Every child must be punished for the parents' sins — unfair across generations
David was born out of wedlock so he laments his mother's sinful conception

content: Psalm 90:10-12 (of Moses) — 'The years of our life are seventy'. What is the prayer at the close of this passage (90:12)?
explanation: Ps 90:12 — 'So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom'. Moses uses life's brevity (70-80 years) as the basis for praying for spiritual wisdom — to value each day.
options: 'Grant that we may live to eighty as our ancestor Abraham lived in his days'
'Grant us wealth and health in our remaining years on this earth'
'TEACH us to NUMBER OUR DAYS that we may get a heart of wisdom'
'Grant us many children and grandchildren to perpetuate our names'

content: Psalm 103:11-13 uses several similes to describe God's mercy. Which images ARE in the text? (select all correct)
explanation: Ps 103:11-13 has three images: (a) heavens above earth (103:11), (b) east from west (103:12), (c) father's compassion (103:13). All describe God's immeasurable mercy. The 'sun after storm' image is fabricated and absent from the text.
options: 'As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us'
'As a FATHER shows compassion to his children, so the LORD shows compassion to those who fear him'
'For as high as the HEAVENS are above the EARTH, so great is his steadfast love toward those who fear him'
'His mercy is like the sun rising after a fierce storm' (not in the text)

content: Psalm 119:105 — 'Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path'. How do the two images 'lamp' and 'light' differ in the verse?
explanation: Psalm 119:105 uses progressive parallelism: 'lamp to my FEET' = illuminating the immediate next step; 'light to my PATH' = illuminating the broader way ahead (the long-term future). The Word guides both present and future.
options: The two images are entirely the same, just a poetic repetition of one idea
The lamp is for Pharisees, the light is for ordinary people in Israel
The lamp is only for the Sabbath, the light is for other workdays of the week
LAMP refers to immediate footstep (my feet); LIGHT refers to the broader path (the way)

content: Psalm 139:13-16 — 'For you formed my inward parts; you knitted me together in my mother's womb'. What wonder of God is celebrated in this passage?
explanation: Ps 139:13-16 — 'I praise you, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made... Your eyes saw my unformed substance; in your book were written, every one of them, the days that were formed for me, when as yet there was none of them'. Truth: God personally knows and cares for each individual from the womb.
options: God's PERSONAL knowledge from conception — he 'sees' and 'writes' all my days before any
God's ability to heal every illness immediately for any individual upon a single prayer
God's power to judge the wicked over the whole world fairly according to his perfect law
God's wisdom in creating all the stars across the heights of heaven from the beginning

content: According to Psalm 1:1 (ESV), 'Blessed is the man' has three negative clauses in a distinctive structure. What is that structure?
explanation: Ps 1:1 — 'Blessed is the man who walks not in the counsel of the wicked, nor stands in the way of sinners, nor sits in the seat of scoffers'. The verbs progress: walks (light pass) → stands (pause) → sits (settle in), paired with three subjects: wicked → sinners → scoffers.
options: 'Does not eat, drink, or sleep' — total abstinence from food and rest entirely
'Walks not in COUNSEL of wicked / stands not in WAY of sinners / sits not in SEAT of scoffers'
'Enters not the temple, offers no sacrifice, attends no festival of the LORD ever'
'Speaks no evil at all, hears no evil at all, thinks no evil at all in any way'

content: According to Psalm 19:14 (ESV), what is the closing prayer? The two blanks: 'Let the ___ of my mouth and the ___ of my heart be acceptable in your sight'.
explanation: Ps 19:14 (ESV) — 'Let the WORDS of my mouth and the MEDITATION of my heart be acceptable in your sight, O LORD, my rock and my redeemer'. The blanks: 'words' and 'meditation' — combining outward speech and inward reflection.

content: According to Psalm 23:1 (ESV), what is the precise opening statement?
explanation: Ps 23:1 (ESV) — 'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want'. The verse emphasizes God's full provision — not necessarily material wealth, but lack of any essential need.
options: 'The LORD is my shepherd; my food shall always be plentiful'
'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall be wealthy all my life'
'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall never die'
'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want'

content: According to Psalm 27:1 (ESV), David declares three titles of the LORD. What are the three?
explanation: Ps 27:1 — 'The LORD is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?' Three titles: light (illumination), salvation (deliverance), stronghold (refuge/strength).
options: 'The LORD is my LIGHT / SALVATION / STRONGHOLD'
'The LORD is my KING / PROPHET / PRIEST'
'The LORD is my FATHER / SON / HOLY SPIRIT'
'The LORD is my BLESSING / GRACE / GLORY'

content: According to Psalm 46:1 (ESV), what is the opening verse? (This psalm inspired Martin Luther's hymn 'A Mighty Fortress'.)
explanation: Ps 46:1 (ESV) — 'God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble'. This verse undergirds the entire psalm and inspired Luther's hymn 'Ein feste Burg' (A Mighty Fortress).
options: 'God is eternal love and the life of all living creatures'
'God is our REFUGE and strength, a very present help in trouble'
'God is the maker of the heavens and the earth from the beginning of creation'
'God is the eternal king ruling over every nation on earth'

content: According to Psalm 51:10-12 (ESV), what does David pray for after his repentance?
explanation: Ps 51:10-12 — 'Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me. Cast me not away from your presence, and take not your Holy Spirit from me. Restore to me the joy of your salvation'. The verb 'create' is bara — used only of God's act of new creation.
options: 'Grant me wealth to compensate Uriah's family for my wrong against them'
'Let me defeat my enemies to prove I am still the chosen king of Israel'
'CREATE IN ME a CLEAN HEART, O God, and renew a right spirit within me'
'Let me live to a hundred years so I have time to amend my life fully'

content: According to Psalm 90:4 (ESV), Moses speaks of the difference between God's time and man's time. What is the precise verse?
explanation: Ps 90:4 — 'For a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday when it is past, or as a watch in the night'. Peter inverts this in 2 Pet 3:8 ('one day as a thousand years') to explain God's delay in judgment.
options: 'A day of the Lord equals a year for man on the earth'
'An hour of the Lord equals the lifetime of man on the earth'
'A minute of the Lord equals all human history since Adam'
'A THOUSAND YEARS in your sight are but as YESTERDAY when it is past, or AS A WATCH IN THE NIGHT'

content: According to Psalm 103:1-2 (ESV), how does David open the psalm with self-exhortation?
explanation: Ps 103:1-2 — 'Bless the LORD, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless his holy name! Bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits'. David addresses his own soul — a spiritual self-exhortation.
options: 'BLESS THE LORD, O MY SOUL, and all that is within me, bless his holy name!'
'Come everyone, bring offerings to the LORD in the temple of Zion'
'O kings of the earth, know that the LORD is king forever'
'O angels in heaven, sing praises to your Creator above the heights'

content: According to Psalm 121:1-2 (ESV), what is the opening of this 'Song of Ascents'?
explanation: Ps 121:1-2 — 'I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come? My help comes from the LORD, who made heaven and earth'. A pilgrim's 'Song of Ascents' going up to Jerusalem — trusting not in hills (sites of false worship) but in the Maker of the hills.
options: 'I lift my eyes to the temples in Zion; my help comes from the high priest there'
'I lift up my eyes to the hills: From where does my help come? My help comes from the LORD, who made heaven and earth'
'I lift my eyes to David's palace; the king will deliver me from all my enemies'
'I lift my eyes to my parents and the ancestors of Abraham's line for my comfort'
